title: SACRAMENTO –
prompt_preview:

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Assembly Bill 2223 strengthens California's role as a reproductive freedom state.",
    "No one will ever be prosecuted or imprisoned in California for a miscarriage, stillbirth, or abortion.",
    "AB 2223 clarifies that the Reproductive Privacy Act prohibits pregnancy criminalization.",
    "AB 2223 creates a private right of action for people whose rights have been violated to seek accountability using civil courts.",
    "The bill removes outdated provisions requiring coroners to investigate certain pregnancy losses.",
    "Information collected about pregnancy loss will not be used to target people through criminal or civil legal systems.",
    "In the past two decades, at least 1,300 pregnant people have been criminally prosecuted for having miscarriages or stillbirths, or self-managing an abortion.",
    "California prosecutors have charged people with homicide offenses for pregnancy losses despite clear law stating that ending or losing a pregnancy is not a crime.",
    "Two women from California's Central Valley spent 4 years and 1 year respectively in prison after delivering stillborn babies.",
    "The misuse of state power in California is partly due to outdated provisions that give coroners the duty to investigate certain abortions and pregnancy losses.",
    "Health care providers and institutions have reported people who have just given birth, had an abortion, or experienced a pregnancy loss to police, triggering harmful investigations and unlawful prosecutions.",
    "AB 2223 creates a shield to separate reproductive healthcare from policing systems that discriminate based on race and wealth.",
    "Growing efforts in California attempt to interrogate, prosecute, and incarcerate people for their pregnancy outcomes."
  ]
}
```
